title: Re-acquiring a non-reentrant lock from the same thread deadlocks against yourself
rule: A plain mutex (Python threading.Lock, std::mutex, pthread default mutex) is non-reentrant: if a thread that already holds it acquires it again, it self-deadlocks (Python/pthread block forever; re-locking std::mutex is undefined behavior, typically a deadlock). This commonly bites when a locked public method calls another locked public method, or in recursion. Either take the lock once at the boundary and have inner helpers assume it's held, or use a reentrant lock (RLock, std::recursive_mutex) deliberately.
triggers: program hangs acquiring a mutex it already holds
std::mutex lock twice undefined behavior deadlock
public method calls public method both take the lock and hang
